title: JIT and AOT Compilation for beamr
intention: beamr interprets BEAM bytecode correctly but slowly. JIT and AOT compilation close the performance gap without sacrificing the properties that make the BEAM valuable: preemptive scheduling, per-process GC, hot code loading, and fault isolation. The interpreter remains the ground truth. Compiled code is an optimisation that the runtime can always fall back from. A workflow author should never know or care whether their code is interpreted or compiled — the observable behaviour is identical.
problem: Interpretation is inherently slower than native execution. Every BEAM opcode dispatches through a match, extracts tagged values, performs the operation, and writes back. For compute-heavy Gleam workflows (AST transforms, dependency graph traversal, large collection processing), this overhead is the bottleneck. The BEAM ecosystem expects JIT-class performance from OTP 24+ — beamr needs parity to be a credible runtime for production Meridian workloads. Additionally, Gleam's static type system erases at the BEAM level, wasting information that could eliminate tag checks, specialise operations, and enable native-width arithmetic.

decisions.title: Cranelift as JIT backend
decisions.chosen: Cranelift (cranelift-codegen + cranelift-module + cranelift-jit) as the single JIT backend.
decisions.rationale: Rust-native, no C++ dependency, designed for fast compilation (not peak optimisation), actively maintained by the Wasmtime team. LLVM would produce better code but compile 10-100x slower — unacceptable for JIT where compilation latency is user-visible.
decisions.rejected: LLVM (too slow for JIT), GCC (no Rust bindings), custom backend (enormous effort, no benefit over Cranelift).

decisions.title: Feature-gated JIT dependency
decisions.chosen: Cranelift dependencies behind a cargo feature flag (beamr/jit). Non-JIT builds pay zero dependency or binary size cost.
decisions.rationale: Cranelift adds ~15 crates. Embedded or minimal deployments should not be forced to carry them. The interpreter is always present as fallback.
decisions.rejected: Always-on dependency (penalises minimal builds), separate crate (splits the compilation pipeline across crate boundaries).

decisions.title: Tiered execution model
decisions.chosen: Interpret first, profile call counts, compile hot functions after a configurable threshold. Compiled code replaces interpreter dispatch for that function. Deoptimise back to interpreter on uncommon paths (exceptions, hot reload invalidation).
decisions.rationale: Matches OTP JIT strategy. Cold functions stay interpreted (no compilation overhead). Hot functions get native code. Deoptimisation keeps the interpreter as the universal fallback.
decisions.rejected: Compile-everything-upfront (slow startup, wasted work on cold paths), trace-based JIT (complex, poor fit for BEAM's function-granularity dispatch).

decisions.title: GC safepoints via stack maps
decisions.chosen: Emit GC safepoints at every GC-possible point in compiled code: allocation sites, function call sites (callee may trigger GC), and yield points. Each safepoint records which machine registers and stack slots hold live GC roots. The garbage collector uses these maps to find and update roots during collection.
decisions.rationale: BEAM GC is per-process and generational. Compiled code must cooperate with the same GC that serves interpreted code. Stack maps are the standard approach (used by JVMs, V8, OTP JIT). Without them, GC cannot safely collect during native execution.
decisions.rejected: Conservative GC (scans entire stack, false positives prevent collection), shadow stack (runtime overhead on every function call).

decisions.title: Mixed-mode execution
decisions.chosen: Compiled and interpreted functions coexist in the same process. The interpreter checks the JIT cache before dispatching each call. Compiled code calls back into the interpreter for un-compiled callees via a runtime helper.
decisions.rationale: Not all functions will be compiled (cold code, unsupported opcodes). The system must handle mixed call stacks gracefully. This is how OTP's JIT works.
decisions.rejected: All-or-nothing compilation (too restrictive), separate native processes (breaks BEAM process model).

decisions.title: Generation-based code invalidation
decisions.chosen: JIT cache entries are keyed by (module, function, arity, generation). purge_module invalidates all compiled code for that module's old generation. force_purge_module invalidates both generations. In-flight calls to old code complete normally; new calls use the current generation.
decisions.rationale: Integrates with beamr's existing purge_module/force_purge_module hot code loading model (current/old two-generation scheme). The JIT cache is an additional layer that must respect the same generation semantics.
decisions.rejected: Separate generation counter (diverges from existing purge model), immediate invalidation (breaks in-flight calls), no invalidation (stale compiled code after reload).

decisions.title: AOT via Gleam type information
decisions.chosen: Two AOT paths: (1) BEAM-to-Cranelift (works with any BEAM language, no type info, same tag checks as JIT), (2) Gleam-to-native (uses Gleam's type annotations to eliminate tag checks, specialise arithmetic, and inline known-safe operations). Both produce the same native code format as JIT.
decisions.rationale: Path 1 is a build-time version of JIT — useful for predictable startup performance. Path 2 is the high-performance path that justifies Gleam as the primary language. Sharing the Cranelift backend between JIT and AOT avoids duplicating the entire code generation pipeline.
decisions.rejected: Gleam-to-Rust transpilation (loses BEAM process model), Gleam-to-WASM (adds unnecessary indirection).

decisions.title: Gleam type sidecar for AOT
decisions.chosen: The Gleam compiler emits a .gleam_types companion file alongside each .beam file. This sidecar contains function signatures, type annotations, and specialisation hints. The AOT pipeline reads both .beam and .gleam_types to drive type-aware code generation.
decisions.rationale: Gleam erases types at the BEAM level — .beam bytecode has no type info. A sidecar file avoids forking the Gleam compiler while preserving the standard gleam build toolchain. The sidecar format is beamr-specific and versioned.
decisions.rejected: Gleam compiler fork (maintenance burden, fragile coupling), debug info section in .beam (non-standard, breaks BEAM loader compatibility), runtime type inference (incomplete, defeats the purpose of static types).

decisions.title: JIT compilation runs on dirty CPU threads
decisions.chosen: JIT compilation runs on the dirty CPU scheduler pool (from B-077). The profiler queues a compilation request; a dirty CPU thread compiles the function via Cranelift; the result is installed in the JIT cache. Normal scheduler threads are never blocked by compilation.
decisions.rationale: Dirty CPU threads already exist and handle compute-heavy work that shouldn't block normal scheduling. JIT compilation is CPU-bound and unbounded in duration — exactly what dirty schedulers are for. A dedicated compilation thread pool would duplicate existing infrastructure.
decisions.rejected: Normal scheduler threads (blocks process scheduling during compilation), dedicated thread pool (duplicates dirty CPU infrastructure), async compilation (Cranelift is synchronous, async wrapper adds complexity without benefit).

decisions.title: Single-binary packaging
decisions.chosen: Embed compiled .beam files (and optionally AOT native code) into the beamr binary as a static asset. The loader checks the embedded archive before the filesystem. Inspired by Bun/Deno single-binary deployments.
decisions.rationale: Deployment simplicity for Meridian agents — one binary, no external .beam files to manage. The embedded archive is a build-time artifact, not a runtime concern.
decisions.rejected: Filesystem-only loading (requires deploying .beam files alongside the binary), dynamic linking (adds deployment complexity).

decisions.title: Differential testing as primary correctness strategy
decisions.chosen: Run every test through both interpreter and JIT/AOT paths. Compare results. Any divergence is a compiler bug. The interpreter is the oracle — it is correct by construction (verified across Phases 0-3).
decisions.rationale: JIT compilers are notoriously hard to test. The interpreter gives us a free oracle. Differential testing catches miscompilations that unit tests miss. Property-based testing and fuzzing complement this by generating inputs the test suite doesn't cover.
decisions.rejected: Unit testing only (insufficient for compiler correctness), formal verification (disproportionate effort for a JIT).

goals: Hot BEAM functions compile to native code via Cranelift with no observable behaviour change versus interpretation.
GC safepoints in compiled code allow per-process garbage collection to proceed without stopping other processes.
Hot code loading invalidates compiled code and falls back to interpretation for the new module generation.
Gleam AOT compilation produces measurably faster execution on statically-typed operations than the untyped JIT path, verified by benchmark comparison.
Single-binary packaging embeds .beam files into the beamr executable for zero-dependency deployment.
Differential testing infrastructure runs every test through both interpreter and compiled paths, failing on divergence.
non_goals: Peak-optimised native code (Cranelift prioritises compilation speed over code quality — this is intentional).
Support for arbitrary BEAM languages beyond Gleam for AOT (JIT works with any .beam, AOT type-specialisation requires Gleam type info).
WASM compilation target (out of scope — beamr targets native only).
Meridian integration changes (B-155 is out of scope — Meridian integrates through Aion, not directly through beamr).
Deterministic replay in this cluster (deferred to a separate brief B-139).

structure.description: All JIT/AOT code lives under crates/beamr/src/jit/, feature-gated behind beamr/jit.

files.description: Feature-gated module root — pub mod + pub use only
JitCompiler: BEAM function -> Cranelift IR -> native code
Call counter, hot function detection, compilation trigger
IR translation: move, swap, arithmetic, comparison opcodes
IR translation: call, return, label, branch, jump table
IR translation: type tests, guard BIFs, select_val
GC safepoint emission, stack map generation
IR translation: tuple and list construction/access
Criterion benchmarks and adaptive threshold tuning
AOT compilation entry point: .beam -> native at build time
Gleam type annotation consumer for AOT specialisation
Single-binary .beam embedding and embedded loader (not JIT-gated)
Differential test runner: interpreter vs compiled

constraints: Compiled code MUST produce identical observable results to the interpreter for all inputs. The interpreter is the correctness oracle.
GC MUST be able to collect any process at any safepoint, regardless of whether the process is running interpreted or compiled code.
Hot code loading MUST invalidate compiled code for the reloaded module. Old-generation compiled code may continue executing but new calls MUST use the new generation.
The JIT feature gate MUST be purely additive — disabling it produces an interpreter-only build with no residual JIT code or dependencies.
Reduction counting MUST work identically in compiled code — a compiled function decrements the reduction counter and yields to the scheduler at the same points as the interpreter.

Deoptimisation MUST preserve all process state (heap, stack, registers, reduction counter) identically to interpreter state at the equivalent bytecode position. Any state inconsistency after deopt is a correctness bug.
Compiled code MUST NOT produce memory accesses outside the process heap. A miscompilation that reads or writes outside the process heap is a sandbox escape.
No file in the jit/ module SHALL exceed 500 lines of production code (per project convention).
